name: Different Modes of Agitation: Growth of Satyagraha and Militant Movements

sourceRefs.label: 2026_q page 90 Q110 (Satyagraha-location matching: Vaikom/Bardoli/Neil Statue/Simon Commission, with answer key)
2024_q page 24 Q30 (Periyar's chronology incl. organizing Vaikom Satyagraha, with answer key)
2022_q page 9 Q31 (Gandhi's influence — Ruskin's 'Unto This Last', with answer key)
Chauri Chaura incident, Dandi March/Salt Satyagraha, Quit India Movement — standard well-established history
vajiramandravi.com, Wikipedia - Individual Satyagraha/Vinoba Bhave, Cripps Mission/'post-dated cheque' quote, Chittagong Armoury Raid/Surya Sen

researchNotes: 8th topic of Unit III. 3 real hits, including a genuinely useful 'match the Satyagraha to its location' question covering 4 different named Satyagrahas in one go. Cross-references Day 22's Periyar content (he organized the Vaikom Satyagraha) without repeating it verbatim. Expanded 2026-07 pass: the original pass covered the Satyagraha strand fairly well but the 'militant movements' strand was thin (one QA). Added Gandhi's first two Indian Satyagrahas (Champaran, Kheda), the Rowlatt Act/Rowlatt Satyagraha/Jallianwala Bagh chain, the Non-Cooperation Movement, and — verified carefully after an initial web-search summary conflated two different organizations — the correct attribution of the Kakori Conspiracy to the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA, which evolved into the HSRA Bhagat Singh joined), kept separate from the earlier, distinct Anushilan Samiti (linked instead to the 1908 Alipore Bomb Case), plus the US-based Ghadar Party. Round-2 pass (2026-07, second expansion): added the Individual Satyagraha (1940-41, Vinoba Bhave as first satyagrahi — the deliberate, limited precursor to Quit India), the Cripps Mission's failure and Gandhi's 'post-dated cheque' verdict, and the Chittagong Armoury Raid (Surya Sen) to round out the militant strand alongside the already-covered HRA/HSRA and Ghadar Party. Roughly a third more mock questions (15→19) — this topic was already fairly dense from round 1, so the increment is smaller than more thinly-covered topics in this batch.

reading.intro: This topic covers the SPECTRUM of resistance — from Gandhi's non-violent Satyagraha (tested via specific named campaigns and their locations) to the militant/revolutionary strand (already covered via Bhagat Singh's HSRA in Day 22). Real exam evidence favors matching named Satyagrahas to their locations.

qa.question: Real exam: Match these movements to their locations — arrival of Simon Commission protest, No-Tax movement, Temple-entry movement, Neil Statue Satyagraha.
qa.answer: Simon Commission protest → Bombay. No-Tax movement → Bardoli (the Bardoli Satyagraha, 1928, against an unjust land-revenue hike). Temple-entry movement → Vaikom (the Vaikom Satyagraha, 1924-25, for lower-caste temple-road access). Neil Statue Satyagraha → Chennai (a TN-specific protest against a colonial-era statue).

qa.question: Real exam: Who organized the Vaikom Satyagraha, and where does this fit in his career chronology?
qa.answer: Thanthai Periyar organized it — chronologically, after he joined Congress but before he became president of the Tamil Nadu Congress Committee, and well before he later founded/led the Justice Party phase of his career.

qa.question: Real exam: Which book influenced Mahatma Gandhi's thinking?
qa.answer: John Ruskin's 'Unto This Last' (an economics/social-critique work that shaped Gandhi's ideas on simple living and economic justice, feeding into his broader Satyagraha philosophy).

qa.question: What was the Chauri Chaura incident (1922), and what was its consequence?
qa.answer: A violent clash on 4 February 1922 in Chauri Chaura (Gorakhpur district) during the Non-Cooperation Movement, where a mob killed 22 policemen. Gandhi held this violated the movement's non-violence pledge and called off the Non-Cooperation Movement as a result, believing the masses weren't yet sufficiently disciplined in Satyagraha.

qa.question: What was the Dandi March (Salt Satyagraha), and what did it trigger?
qa.answer: A 240-mile, 24-day non-violent march (12 March - 5 April 1930) led by Gandhi and 78 followers from Sabarmati Ashram to the coastal village of Dandi, Gujarat. On 6 April, Gandhi symbolically broke the Salt Act by making salt from seawater — this triggered the broader Civil Disobedience Movement, leading to over 60,000 arrests including Gandhi's own (5 May 1930).

qa.question: What was the Quit India Movement (1942)?
qa.answer: A mass uprising launched by Gandhi and Congress during World War II, demanding immediate British withdrawal ('Quit India'). Planned as non-violent, it turned violent in places after the British arrested Congress leaders almost immediately.

qa.question: How does the 'militant movements' strand differ from Satyagraha, and what's a key example already covered?
qa.answer: Militant/revolutionary nationalism rejected strict non-violence, favoring direct action (including armed resistance) against British rule — e.g., Bhagat Singh's Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA, covered in the Leaders II topic), which carried out the Saunders assassination and was tried in the Lahore Conspiracy Case.

qa.question: What were the Champaran (1917) and Kheda (1918) Satyagrahas, Gandhi's first on Indian soil?
qa.answer: Champaran (Bihar): against the 'tinkathia' system that forced peasants to grow indigo on part of their land for British landlords. Kheda (Gujarat): sought tax relief for farmers hit by crop failure, and drew young nationalists like Sardar Patel into Gandhi's fold. Both preceded his larger national campaigns and followed his earlier Satyagraha experience in South Africa.

qa.question: What was the Rowlatt Act (1919), and how did Gandhi respond?
qa.answer: A repressive law (formally the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act) granting the British government power to arrest and imprison without trial for up to 2 years. Gandhi launched the Rowlatt Satyagraha, pledging civil disobedience against it — the resulting unrest culminated in the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre (13 April 1919, Amritsar).

qa.question: What was the Non-Cooperation Movement (1920–22), and what triggered it?
qa.answer: A mass movement of boycotting British goods, titles, institutions, and legislatures, notable for formally fusing the nationalist movement with the Khilafat Movement. Triggered by the combined grievances of WWI's aftermath, the Rowlatt Act, and the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre.

qa.question: What was the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA), and what was its most famous action?
qa.answer: Founded in October 1924 by Ram Prasad Bismil and Sachindranath Sanyal to pursue full independence through armed revolution. Its most famous action was the Kakori Conspiracy (9 August 1925) — a train robbery near Lucknow to fund weapons; Bismil and others were later executed. The HRA subsequently evolved into the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association (HSRA) — the same organization Bhagat Singh joined in 1928.
qa.tip: Chain the organizations chronologically: HRA (1924, Kakori) → renamed/evolved into HSRA (1928, Bhagat Singh's organization) — they're the same lineage, not two unrelated groups.

qa.question: What was the Anushilan Samiti, and how does it differ from the HRA/HSRA?
qa.answer: An earlier revolutionary secret society founded in Bengal in the early 1900s, associated with figures like Aurobindo Ghosh and linked to the 1908 Alipore Bomb Case — predating the HRA/HSRA generation by roughly two decades. Keep them separate: Anushilan Samiti = early-1900s Bengal; HRA/HSRA = mid-1920s onward, Kakori/Bhagat Singh.

qa.question: What was the Ghadar Party, and where was it founded?
qa.answer: Founded in 1913 by Indian immigrants (mainly Punjabi) in San Francisco, USA, aiming to overthrow British rule in India through armed rebellion — representing the militant movement's international dimension outside India itself.

qa.question: Round-2 pass — What was the Individual Satyagraha (1940-41), and who was chosen as its first satyagrahi?
qa.answer: A deliberately limited, symbolic form of civil disobedience launched by Gandhi in October 1940 (during WWII, before the more mass-scale Quit India Movement), in which hand-picked individuals, one at a time, publicly courted arrest by opposing India's forced participation in the war. Acharya Vinoba Bhave was chosen as the first individual satyagrahi (17 October 1940, at Paunar near Wardha); Jawaharlal Nehru was the second.

qa.question: What was the Cripps Mission (March 1942), and what was Gandhi's famous verdict on it?
qa.answer: A British mission led by Sir Stafford Cripps offering Dominion Status AFTER World War II ended, in exchange for full Indian cooperation during the war — rejected by Congress as inadequate (it didn't offer immediate self-government) and by the Muslim League (over the lack of a clear partition provision). Gandhi famously dismissed the offer as 'a post-dated cheque drawn on a failing/crashing bank'.

qa.question: What was the Chittagong Armoury Raid (1930), and who led it?
qa.answer: Led by Surya Sen ('Masterda'), a coordinated raid on 18 April 1930 attempting to seize the police and auxiliary-forces armouries at Chittagong (Bengal, now Bangladesh) and cut telegraph/rail links, as part of the militant revolutionary strand alongside the HRA/HSRA and Ghadar Party. Surya Sen evaded capture for years but was eventually caught and executed by the British on 12 January 1934.

questions.prompt: The Vaikom Satyagraha (temple-entry movement) took place in:
The Bardoli Satyagraha is best known as a:
options: No-tax movement against an unjust revenue hike
questions.explanation: A no-tax movement (1928), against a land-revenue increase.

questions.prompt: Which book by John Ruskin influenced Mahatma Gandhi's thinking?
The Chauri Chaura incident (1922) directly led Gandhi to:
questions.explanation: He called off the Non-Cooperation Movement.

questions.prompt: The Dandi March (1930) directly protested which British law?
The Quit India Movement (1942) was launched during which global conflict?
The Rowlatt Act (1919) granted the British government the power to:
options: Arrest and imprison without trial for up to 2 years
questions.explanation: Arrest and imprison without trial for up to 2 years.

questions.prompt: Gandhi's Rowlatt Satyagraha is most directly linked to which tragic event?
The Champaran Satyagraha (1917) was against which exploitative agricultural system?
The Non-Cooperation Movement (1920–22) formally fused the nationalist movement with which other movement?
The Kakori Conspiracy (1925) was carried out by which organization?
questions.explanation: Hindustan Republican Association (HRA), founded by Ram Prasad Bismil and Sachindranath Sanyal.
questions.trick: Don't confuse HRA (1924, Kakori) with the earlier, separate Anushilan Samiti (1900s Bengal) — different generations of militant organizations.

questions.prompt: The Hindustan Republican Association (HRA) later evolved into which organization — the same one Bhagat Singh joined in 1928?
The Anushilan Samiti, an early revolutionary secret society, was based in which region and linked to which case?
questions.explanation: Bengal, linked to the 1908 Alipore Bomb Case.

questions.prompt: The Ghadar Party (founded 1913) was established by Indian immigrants based in which country?
Who was chosen as the FIRST individual satyagrahi in Gandhi's Individual Satyagraha movement of October 1940?
questions.explanation: Acharya Vinoba Bhave, on 17 October 1940 at Paunar; Nehru was second.

questions.prompt: Gandhi famously dismissed the Cripps Mission's (1942) offer of post-war Dominion Status as:
options: "A post-dated cheque drawn on a failing/crashing bank"
questions.explanation: "A post-dated cheque drawn on a failing/crashing bank."

questions.prompt: The Chittagong Armoury Raid (18 April 1930) was led by:
questions.explanation: Surya Sen, executed by the British on 12 January 1934.

questions.prompt: The Individual Satyagraha (1940-41) was, by design, different from the later Quit India Movement in that it was:
options: A deliberately limited, symbolic protest by hand-picked individuals one at a time
Led entirely by revolutionaries, not Gandhi
questions.explanation: A deliberately limited, symbolic form of civil disobedience — one individual at a time, courting arrest.
